session.name: RunPod remote Cubric engine brainstorm
session.branch: unknown - git state intentionally not checked during handoff

goal.original: Brainstorm whether Cubric Vision should support RunPod so users without strong local GPUs can run the Comfy engine remotely, using user-provided RunPod credentials rather than a Cubric auth system.
goal.summary: Initial investigation found that a generic remote ComfyUI URL is not sufficient because Cubric requires specific ComfyUI versions, custom nodes, workflows, lifecycle control, and reliable video support. The current direction is a Cubric-managed RunPod Pod using an approved Cubric template, persistent network volume, user-selected GPU, and app-managed start/stop lifecycle.

completed: Read project routing docs, ComfyUI docs/rules, project knowledge index, and relevant controller/executor/settings code.
Identified the existing execution choke point: generationService -> commandExecutor -> ComfyUIController.runWorkflow.
Checked current RunPod docs for Pods, templates, pod create/start/stop, exposed ports, and storage/network volumes.
Revised the design away from arbitrary remote ComfyUI endpoints toward a Cubric-owned remote engine template.
pending: Continue investigation of RunPod templates, network volume behavior across GPU changes, API lifecycle, secure transport/auth, and template versioning.
Investigate OneTrainer's RunPod integration as a proven open-source reference if available locally or on GitHub.
Decide whether the remote service should proxy native ComfyUI endpoints or expose a Cubric wrapper API with token auth and compatibility checks.
After design approval, create a JSON board idea task, then create a plan.

allowed_actions: investigate RunPod docs and OneTrainer reference
create board task only after user approves design direction

completed: Captured the revised product constraints and current investigation findings in this handoff.
No code files were modified during the brainstorm.
pending: If the design is approved, use mpi-brainstorm's board-capture step before asking whether to create a plan.
Consider preserving durable RunPod design decisions in project memory only after user approval or after implementation decisions are settled.

next_action.description: Resume the brainstorm by deepening the RunPod investigation around Cubric-owned templates, network volumes, GPU/PyTorch compatibility gating, secure connection options including HTTP proxy vs SSH/tunnel, and OneTrainer's implementation pattern.

key_decisions: Generic remote ComfyUI URL is not an acceptable v1 because Cubric owns specific ComfyUI version, custom nodes, workflows, and lifecycle requirements.
The preferred v1 direction is Cubric-managed RunPod Pods created from a hidden approved Cubric template; the user supplies a RunPod API key and selects a GPU.
Cubric must start/create the remote service before generation and stop/delete the Pod on app quit so the user does not keep paying for GPU time.
Persistent storage should be based on RunPod network volumes so models/custom nodes/cache survive Pod deletion and later GPU/POD changes.
Video support is a primary driver, so an image-only remote MVP is not useful for this feature.
constraints: User does not want a Cubric auth system; user-provided RunPod API key is acceptable.
Template is hidden from normal UX; Cubric uses an approved/community template in the background when talking to RunPod.
Remote engine must match Cubric's ComfyUI version, custom nodes, workflow requirements, and likely workflow bundle/version manifest.

PyTorch/CUDA/GPU compatibility is a core design issue: a volume initialized for one GPU generation may not be compatible with another. The app likely needs to remember the installed torch/CUDA profile and gate later GPU choices with clear feedback.
Need investigate whether RunPod HTTP proxy is acceptable with auth or whether SSH/tunneling is safer/needed.

RunPod docs consulted during initial session: templates overview, Pod create/start/stop API, storage types/network volumes, exposed ports, and serverless request model. Prefer primary RunPod docs in the next session.
OneTrainer is a user-cited proven reference for similar RunPod automation; user believes it may be open source and may be installed locally.

resume_prompt: Read .agents/mpi-kanban/state/handoffs/d46242e5-3e0b-4fbf-ba56-6e049bf6f3b4.json and use mpi-continue to continue the RunPod remote Cubric engine brainstorm. Continue from the revised direction: Cubric-managed RunPod Pod from an approved hidden template, user API key, GPU selection, network volume persistence, app-managed start/stop on launch/quit, PyTorch/CUDA compatibility gating, secure connection choice, and OneTrainer as a reference to investigate.
